paper_title: Visual Aesthetic Benchmark: Can Frontier Models Judge Beauty?

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) are now routinely deployed for visual understanding, generation, and curation. A substantial fraction of these applications require an explicit aesthetic judgment. Most existing solutions reduce this judgment to predicting a scalar score for a single image. We first ask whether such scores faithfully capture comparative preference: in a controlled study with eight expert annotators, score-derived rankings align poorly with the same annotators' direct comparisons, while direct ranking yields substantially higher inter-annotator agreement on best- and worst-image labels. Motivated by this finding, we introduce the Visual Aesthetic Benchmark (VAB), which casts aesthetic evaluation as comparative selection over candidate sets with matched subject matter. VAB contains 400 tasks and 1,195 images across fine art, photography, and illustration, with labels derived from the consensus of 10 independent expert judges per task. Evaluating 20 frontier MLLMs and six dedicated visual-quality reward models, we find that the strongest system identifies both the best and the worst image correctly across three random permutations of the candidate order in only 26.5% of tasks, far below the 68.9% achieved by human experts. Fine-tuning a 35B-parameter model on 2,000 expert examples brings its accuracy close to that of a 397B-parameter open-weight model, suggesting that the comparative signal in VAB is transferable. Together, these results expose a clear and measurable gap between current multimodal models and expert aesthetic judgment, and VAB provides the first set-based, expert-grounded testbed on which that gap can be tracked and closed.

sections.title: 1 Introduction
sections.content: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have become critical to widening range of visual tasks such as image generation and curation (Liu et al., 2023; Team et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2024). Many of these applications require aesthetic judgment, asking models to compare visual works and identify differences in execution quality across composition, color harmony, technical execution, and cultural convention. This makes aesthetic judgment demanding test of higher-order multimodal reasoning that is often omitted by existing visual benchmarks (Yu et al., 2023; Yue et al., 2024). Existing image-aesthetics datasets and methods typically reduce aesthetic judgment to mapping from an image to scalar score (Murray et al., 2012; Kong et al., 2016; He et al., 2022; Lee and Kim, 2019; Hosu et al., 2019; Yi et al., 2023; He et al., 2023), an approach that we argue is poorly suited to the underlying decision being studied. Preference order is inferred from independent scores rather than measured through direct comparison; crowd-averaged labels mix expertise, taste, and annotation noise, so the aesthetic signal is difficult to isolate; and because images in such datasets often depict different subjects, model can shortcut its way to high accuracy by exploiting content preferences instead of discriminating execution quality. We first ask whether scoring faithfully captures comparative preference . Eight expert annotators evaluate the same images under both independent absolute scoring and direct comparative ranking, and the two 1 BakeAI Introduction Figure 1: Overview of the VAB benchmark. VAB spans three visual domains fine art, photography, and illustration covering 24 topics across 400 evaluation tasks and 1,195 images. Each task presents set of candidate images sharing common subject. protocols disagree. Comparative ranking yields 42 percentage points higher inter-annotator agreement on best-image selection than score-derived rankings (Talebi and Milanfar, 2018; Ma et al., 2017; Wu et al., 2023; Zhou et al., 2024a). The two protocols therefore elicit different preference signals from the same annotators, and direct comparison captures the underlying judgment more faithfully than the pointwise scoring used in most existing pipelines. Building on this finding, we introduce the Visual Aesthetic Benchmark (VAB), which formulates aesthetic judgment as comparative selection problem over candidate sets with matched subject matter. VAB contains 400 tasks and 1,195 images across fine art, photography, and illustration, covering 24 topics (Figure 1). Each task presents two to six images sharing common subject, and the model selects the best image and, under the stricter setting, also the worst image. Three design choices distinguish VAB from prior aesthetic benchmarks. Matched subject matter within each task isolates differences in execution from differences in content; each ground-truth label is the consensus of 10 independent expert judges, filtered through dualthreshold criterion to remove ambiguous cases; and every task is evaluated under three independent random permutations of the candidate order. We report ap@1, the average accuracy across the three evaluations, and pass^3, which credits task only when the model is correct in all three. We benchmark 20 frontier MLLMs and six dedicated visual-quality reward models on VA B. The strongest system, Claude Sonnet 4.6, reaches 26.5% on TB-1 pass^3, 42.4 points below the human baseline of 68.9%. Models are also highly sensitive to candidate order, retaining only 3465% of their ap@1 accuracy when required to be correct across all three permutations, and they degrade much faster than humans as the candidate set grows: 7.1 drop from two-image to four-or-more-image tasks, against only 2.0 for human experts. The contributions of this paper are as follows. controlled human study with eight expert annotators establishes that direct comparative ranking is substantially more reliable than independent scoring for subjective aesthetic annotation, and motivates the design of the benchmark. 2 BakeAI 2 Human Annotation Study: Ranking vs. Scoring VAB is, to our knowledge, the first set-based, expert-grounded, multi-domain aesthetic benchmark with permutation-robust evaluation, covering 400 tasks and 1,195 images across fine art, photography, and illustration. systematic evaluation of 20 frontier MLLMs and six dedicated reward models exposes persistent 42.4-point gap to human experts on TB-1 pass^3 together with severe positional instability across permutations of the candidate order. Fine-tuning on 2,000 expert examples brings 35B-parameter model close to 397B-parameter openweight model, indicating that expert comparative judgments transfer effectively through lightweight adaptation.

sections.title: 2 Human Annotation Study: Ranking vs. Scoring
sections.content: Existing aesthetic benchmarks rely on Scoring to collect ground truth labels (Murray et al., 2012; Kong et al., 2016; He et al., 2022; Cao et al., 2025). We conduct controlled human study to explore the following research question regarding the annotation process: Which protocol yields more scientifically sound signal for data annotation? Scoring: expert annotates each image independently on an absolute aesthetic scale. Ranking: expert compares images and annotates their relative order. Setup and Metrics. Eight expert annotators evaluate the same images under two protocols: (1) scoring assigns each image an independent score si,a [0, 10]; (2) ranking orders images directly within groups of size {2, 3, 4, 5}. We construct 119 homogeneous-content tasks, in which all candidates share the same underlying content, and 107 heterogeneous-content tasks formed by regrouping images across source groups while preserving topic consistency. We compare the two protocols along three dimensions: Fidelity (Kendalls τ (Kendall, 1938)): we compute τ = (C D)/(n 2), where and are the numbers of concordant and discordant pairs between an annotators score-derived ranking σscore and the same annotators direct comparison. low τ means the two protocols elicit different preference orderings from the same person. We additionally report the top-1 self-consistency (SC) rate: the fraction of annotatortask pairs for which σscore and the direct comparison select the same top image. Reproducibility (accbest/worst): accbest is the proportion of tasks where single image receives 5 of 8 best-image votes; accworst and accbest&worst are defined analogously. Higher accuracy means the protocol produces ground truth labels that different experts can independently reproduce. Distinguishability (top-1 entropy (Shannon, 1948)): we measure = pi log2 pi, where pi is the fraction of annotators selecting image as best. Lower entropy means annotators concentrate on single top choice rather than spreading votes. Full protocol details, notations, formal definitions, and statistical tests are in Appendix F. Scoring does not faithfully preserve preference order. Table 1 shows that score-derived rankings often diverge from the direct comparisons given by the same annotator. The mean Kendalls τ is 0.188 for homogeneous-content and 0.351 for heterogeneous-content tasks; the top-1 self-consistency rates are 45.5% and 52.0%, respectively. In other words, nearly half of the numeric score annotations fail to recover the order that the same annotator expresses under direct comparison. Ranking yields more reproducible labels. Table 2 shows that ranking yields substantially higher interannotator agreement on best and worst labels. On homogeneous-content tasks, ranking improves accbest by 42.0 points, accworst by 37.0 points, and accbest&worst by 51.3 points (all < 0.001, McNemar test (McNemar, 3 BakeAI 3 The VAB Benchmark Table 1: We report within-annotator agreement between experts score-derived rankings σscore and direct comparison from 8 annotators, measured by Kendalls τ () and top-1 self-consistency rate (SC rate, ), defined as the fraction of annotatortask pairs where the score-derived top-1 matches the direct comparison top-1. The results show that nearly 50% of the numeric score annotations do not reliably preserve the order that the annotator would express in direct comparison. (a) Results on homogeneous-content tasks. (b) Results on heterogeneous-content tasks. 3 4 5 tasks Mean τ Std. τ SC rate 28 38 35 18 0.098 0. 0.126 0.267 0.511 0.328 0.243 0. 54.9% 49.7% 34.3% 43.6% 3 4 5 tasks Mean τ Std. τ SC rate 19 37 34 17 0.342 0. 0.441 0.324 0.427 0.300 0.172 0. 67.1% 48.0% 51.1% 45.6% All 0.188 0.354 45.5% All 107 0. 0.291 52.0% Table 2: We report inter-annotator consistency on best and worst decisions for 8 annotators, where majority requires agreement from at least 5 of 8. Each row shows task-level accuracy acc (), improvement , and the exact two-sided McNemar p-value () (McNemar, 1947; Fagerland et al., 2013). The Significance level is marked as < 0.001. Results show that ranking is more likely to yield annotation labels that independent experts can reproduce. Homogeneous-content (N = 119) Heterogeneous-content (N = 107) Metric Scoring Ranking Scoring Ranking accbest accworst accbest&worst 52.9% 55.5% 39.5% 95.0% 92.4% 90.8% +42.0% <0.001 +37.0% <0.001 +51.3% <0.001 65.4% 69.2% 49.5% 94.4% 93.5% 90.7% +29.0% <0.001 +24.3% <0.001 +41.1% <0.001 1947)); the same pattern holds for heterogeneous-content tasks. Independent experts can therefore reproduce best/worst labels far more reliably under ranking than under scoring. Ranking makes the top choice stand out more clearly. Table 3 measures how concentrated their topchoice preference is within each task. It shows that ranking produces lower entropy over top-1 selections (Shannon, 1948) (0.729 vs. 1.213) and much higher rate of unanimous choices (10.1% vs. 0.8%; Wilcoxon = 1.7 1015 (Wilcoxon, 1945)). More generally, ranking concentrates votes on single preferred image rather than spreading them across several plausible winners. Interpretation. Our findings align with conclusions from two complementary disciplines. Aesthetically, modern aesthetics favors relational view of beauty (Goldman, 1995; Levinson, 2005): people judge preference more reliably when alternatives are juxtaposed than when items are assessed in isolation. Cognitively, comparative judgment is more primitive operation than absolute evaluation (Thurstone, 1927). Absolute scoring requires maintaining an internal reference scale and converting qualitative impressions into numbers, imposing heavier cognitive load and greater instability (Stewart et al., 2005; Parducci, 1965). We therefore adopt ranking as the annotation protocol for VA B. Extended discussion is provided in Appendix F.2.

sections.title: 3 The VAB Benchmark
sections.content: Building on the findings of Section 2, we construct the Visual Aesthetic Benchmark (VAB) to test whether frontier multimodal models can make expert-aligned aesthetic judgments under ranking-based protocol. In contrast with prior evaluations that aggregate scalar ratings from crowd annotators (Murray et al., 2012; Kong et al., 2016; He et al., 2022), VA casts aesthetic evaluation as comparative selection over candidate 4 BakeAI 3 The VAB Benchmark Table 3: We report top-choice concentration in the homogeneous-content setting (119 tasks, 8 annotators), measured by entropy (Shannon, 1948) and the rate of unanimous tasks (H = 0). The 95% confidence intervals use normal approximation for mean and Wilson interval (Wilson, 1927) for the unanimous-task rate. Wilcoxon signed-rank test (Wilcoxon, 1945) yields that the difference is statistically significant (p = 1.7 1015). Metric Scoring Ranking Mean () [95% CI] Unanimous rate () 1.213 [1.137, 1.289] 0.8% [0.1%, 4.6%] 0.729 [0.665, 0.793] 10.1% [5.9%, 16.8%] sets with matched subject matter: each task asks the model to identify the best image and, in the stricter setting, also the worst. The remainder of this section describes the task layout, the data construction pipeline, and the ground-truth formation procedure.

sections.title: 3.1 Overview
sections.content: Task formulation. Each benchmark task presents candidate set of images with matched subject matter, so performance depends on differences in execution rather than differences in depicted content. VA contains 400 released tasks and 1,195 images across fine art, photography, and illustration, covering 24 topics with candidate set sizes from 2 to 6 images. We consider two settings: in Top-1, the model selects the best image in the set; in TB-1, it identifies both the best and the worst image. Figure 1 provides visual overview, Figure 2 summarizes the released domain and topic distribution, and Appendix B.1 reports detailed benchmark statistics. Figure 2: Detailed distribution of VAB. Evaluation protocol. Labels are derived from direct comparative judgments by independent experts rather than post hoc aggregation of absolute scores. Each task is evaluated three times, each under an independent random permutation of the candidate order. We report ap@1, the average accuracy across the three evaluations, and pass^3, which counts task as correct only if the model is correct in all three. Representative topic-level examples are collected in Appendix I.

sections.title: 3.2 Data Construction
sections.content: Because VAB is annotated through direct ranking over candidate sets, data construction is organized around matched image groups rather than standalone images: in every domain, the members of set share subject matter but vary in execution quality. Figure 3 summarizes the pipeline; Appendix B.2 reports the full raw-pool statistics, and Appendix B.3 contains the domain-level construction details. Fine Art. We commissioned 426 painting sets comprising 1,142 images. Each set is built from constrained artist prompts that preserve subject matter while allowing variation in composition, value structure, color handling, and finish. All works were commissioned specifically for VAB. Photography. We constructed 670 photography sets comprising 1,809 images. Each set originates from single source photograph, many provided directly by professional photographers, and is expanded through two pipelines: an expert-edit pipeline based on professional retouching tools, which can produce either original/improved pairs or larger multi-expert sets, and scalable agent-edit pipeline that generates improved and degraded variants. The agent-edit prompt sequence and runtime pipeline are recorded in Appendix H.1. 5 BakeAI 3 The VAB Benchmark Figure 3: Overview of the VAB data construction pipeline. Images are organized into candidate sets with matched subject matter and varying aesthetic execution, then passed through expert review and consensus filtering before entering the benchmark. Illustration. We constructed 271 illustration sets comprising 908 images. These sets are formed either by prompt-based generation with text-to-image models or by rendering CC0 3D assets under controlled viewpoint and presentation changes, producing multiple images with matched subject matter but varying execution. Prompt templates are in Appendix H.2. All collected sets are deduplicated and reviewed for semantic consistency before annotation.

sections.title: 3.3 Expert Annotation and Ground Truth
sections.content: Following Section 2, VAB uses ranking-based expert consensus for ground truth. Review setup. Creation and evaluation are strictly separated. In the raw pre-filter pool, each collected candidate set is reviewed by 10 independent expert judges drawn from pool of more than 100 evaluators, yielding over 13,000 assessments and more than 300 hours of review. Judges remain blind to artist identity throughout annotation. Rubrics and interface examples are in Appendix C. Additional topic-level examples are in Appendix I. Annotation protocol. Each task presents candidate images side by side. Judges first assess each image against the domain-specific rubric and then make comparative selection over the full set. For = 2, judge selects the best image. For 3, judge selects both the best and the worst image, with distinct choices required. Every task receives exactly 10 judgments. representative annotation example is shown in Figure 4. 6 BakeAI 4 Experiments Figure 4: Example of single VAB annotation task, illustrating the candidate set presented to expert judges and the resulting consensus decision. The consensus threshold is determined through statistical confidence; see Appendix for details. Consensus filter. task is retained only if expert votes are sufficiently concentrated. For = 2, the best-image votes must meet the threshold; for 3, the best-image and worst-image votes must both meet it, so satisfying only one is insufficient. The thresholds are chosen by statistical confidence under uniform-random null, and the exact derivation and threshold table are provided in Appendix D.

sections.title: 4.1 Setup
sections.content: Evaluation scope. We evaluate 20 MLLMs and six reward models on VA B. Unless otherwise noted, each model is evaluated under its default inference configuration. We compare all systems against two baselines. The Human Expert baseline is obtained from fresh round of annotation in which 10 expert judges evaluate each released task under the benchmark interface, and we report their average accuracy against the released labels. The Random Guess baseline is computed analytically for each task size k. MLLMs. Our MLLM evaluation covers 20 closed-source and open-weight models spanning the Claude family (Anthropic, 2026a; 2025a; 2026b; 2025b; 2026c), the Gemini family (Google DeepMind, 2025b;c; 2026), GPT and o-series models (OpenAI, 2025a;b;c;d;e;f), and additional systems from Doubao, Grok, GLM, Kimi, and Qwen (ByteDance Seed, 2026; xAI, 2025; Z.ai, 2025; Team et al., 2026; Bai et al., 2023; Qwen Team, 2026). For each task, the model selects either the best image (Top-1) or both the best and worst images (TB-1). We evaluate each task three times under independent random permutations of candidate order and report ap@1 and pass^3 as defined in Section 3. Prompts are provided in Appendix H.3. Reward Models. We additionally evaluate six reward models: ArtiMuse (Cao et al., 2025), PEAS-aes and PEAS-ava (Yun and Choo, 2024), Q-Align (Wu et al., 2023), Q-Instruct (Wu et al., 2024), and RealQA-ava (Li et al., 2025). Unlike MLLMs, these models assign scalar scores to images independently rather than selecting directly from set. We therefore follow each models official scoring interface, score each image three times, average the scores, and then rank images within each task. sections.title: 4.2 Results
sections.content: Tables 4 and 5 summarize the main results; Appendices E.1E.4 provide the full tables, topic-level breakdowns, and auxiliary metrics.

sections.title: 4.2.1 Results for Multimodal Large Language Models
sections.content: Gap to experts. Current frontier models remain far below expert performance. On the strictest metric, TB-1 pass^3, Claude Sonnet 4.6 attains 26.5%, against 68.9% for human experts; even under the more lenient TB-1 ap@1, the strongest model, Doubao Seed 2.0 Pro, reaches 42.8%, still 26.1 points short of the expert baseline. Progress across families. Improvements are uneven across model families. Claude Sonnet 4.6 nearly doubles its predecessor on TB-1 pass^3 (14.5% 26.5%), whereas the GPT-5 series regresses from 21.8% (GPT-5) to 20.0% (GPT-5.1) and 15.5% (GPT-5.2). clear closed/open gap persists under the stricter metric: the strongest open-weight system, Qwen3.5-397B-A17B, reaches 17.2% on TB-1 pass^3, compared with 26.5% for Claude Sonnet 4.6. Domain difficulty and stability. Accuracy varies strongly across domains and across repeated evaluations. Illustration is the most difficult domain, where Claude Sonnet 4.6 attains only 19.0% on TB-1 pass^3, while photography is comparatively more tractable, with o4-mini reaching 30.2%. sections.title: 4.2.2 Results for Reward Models
sections.content: Overall performance. Reward models also remain well below human experts, although some are competitive with MLLMs on Top-1. Q-Align is the strongest overall, reaching 52.2% on Top-1 and 38.2% on TB-1, against 77.7% and 68.9% for the human baseline. Domain trends and asymmetric failure modes. Domain trends broadly mirror the MLLM results. Appendix E.2 additionally reveals an asymmetry between best and worst predictions: Q-Instruct reaches 51.5% on overall Bot-1 and 77.0% on photography Bot-1, despite considerably weaker Top-1 scores of 38.5% overall and 43.9% in photography. This pattern suggests that detecting aesthetic failure is in some cases easier than identifying the strongest image in set. Comparison with MLLMs. The best reward-model Top-1 result , 52.2%, is numerically close to the best MLLM Top-1 ap@1 result , 55.4%, but the comparison is not strictly like-for-like: MLLMs make single comparative decision over candidate set, whereas reward models score images independently and are then ranked within the set.

sections.title: 4.3 Ablations and Additional Analysis
sections.content: Temperature ablation. For models with an exposed temperature parameter, greedy decoding improves Top-1 pass^3 by 1.1 points on average and TB-1 pass^3 by 2.3 points. Decoding stochasticity therefore contributes noise, but it does not explain the main performance gap; full model-by-model results are reported in Appendix E.5. Candidate set size. Performance degrades sharply as the candidate set grows. Under TB-1 pass^3, the best model falls from 47.3% on two-image tasks to 6.7% on four-image tasks, while human experts decline from 87.1% to 43.6%. Larger candidate sets therefore amplify both comparative difficulty and instability; the full breakdown is reported in Appendix E.6.

sections.title: 5 Fine-Tuning on Expert Aesthetic Judgments
sections.content: Training Setup. We fine-tune N3.5-35B-A3B using Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) (Hu et al., 2022) with rank 64 and α = 128, freezing the vision tower while updating the multimodal projector. Supervision comes from 2,000 additional expert-annotated examples collected with the same annotation pipeline described in Section 3.3; these examples are fully disjoint from the benchmark evaluation set. We train for three epochs with learning rate of 1 104 and cosine scheduling. Additional hyperparameters and full fine-tuning results are provided in Appendix G. The fine-tuned model is denoted as L I-35BA3B. 9 BakeAI 6 Conclusion Table 6: Fine-tuning results on VA B. Fine-tuning yields consistent gains across all four reported metrics: Top-1 pass^3 improves from 25.5% to 29.5%, TB-1 pass^3 from 15.8% to 17.5%, Top-1 ap@1 from 47.5% to 51.3%, and TB-1 ap@1 from 36.5% to 38.8%. The topic-level breakdown is reported in Appendix G.2. Scaling implication. L I - 3 5 - 3 approaches the substantially larger Qwen3.5-397B-A17B on Top-1 pass^3, 29.5% versus 29.8%, and lies within roughly one point on the other metrics. This is preliminary evidence that the comparative supervision in VAB carries enough aesthetic signal to compensate for an order-of-magnitude reduction in parameter count under lightweight adaptation.

sections.title: 6 Conclusion
sections.content: We introduced VAB, benchmark that recasts aesthetic evaluation as comparative selection over candidate sets with matched subject matter, with labels grounded in expert ranking rather than scalar scoring. VA contains 400 tasks and 1,195 images spanning fine art, photography, and illustration. controlled human study motivates the protocol: direct ranking yields 42 percentage points higher inter-annotator agreement than score-derived rankings. Across 20 frontier MLLMs and six reward models, the strongest system reaches 26.5% on TB-1 pass^3 against 68.9% for human experts, and accuracy drops sharply as the candidate set grows; models also exhibit pronounced positional instability across permutations of the candidate order. Fine-tuning 35B-parameter model on 2,000 expert examples brings its accuracy close to that of 397B-parameter open-weight model, suggesting that expert comparative supervision transfers efficiently with lightweight adaptation. We see several directions for future work: broadening domain and annotator diversity beyond the present three domains, improving robustness to candidate-order permutations, and scaling expert comparative supervision to larger training corpora. By making expert aesthetic judgment measurable, comparative target, VA establishes the foundation on which expert-aligned multimodal aesthetic judgment can now be systematically pursued.

sections.title: Ethics Statement
sections.content: This study involved human participants providing aesthetic judgments through an online evaluation platform. The University of Washington Human Subjects Division (HSD) reviewed the protocol and determined that the research qualifies as exempt human subjects research (Category 2 and 3; minimal-risk behavioral research) under U.S. federal regulations. Participation was voluntary and participants could stop at any time. No sensitive personal information was collected. Only aggregated benchmark results are publicly released, and no identifiable participant data are included in any publications or datasets. The exempt determination letter is on file with the authors. 10 BakeAI References All fine art works were created as commissioned works for VA B, with participating artists informed of and consenting to the use of their work for research. Professional photographers whose images served as source material for the photography pipeline contributed their work under appropriate licensing terms. Agent-edited variants derived from these source photographs were produced within the scope of those agreements. Illustration sets generated through text-to-image models used structured prompts without reproducing copyrighted material, and 3D-rendered sets were constructed exclusively from CC0-licensed assets. We are committed to crediting and fairly compensating all human creators whose work contributed to this benchmark.

sections.title: A Related Work
sections.content: Multimodal Large Language Models. Recent MLLMs such as Flamingo, BLIP-2, InstructBLIP, LLaVA, Gemini, and Qwen2-VL have rapidly improved multimodal perception, instruction following, and reasoning (Alayrac et al., 2022; Li et al., 2023; Dai et al., 2023; Liu et al., 2023; Team et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2024). Their capabilities are typically assessed on broad benchmarks such as MM-Vet and MMMU (Yu et al., 2023; Yue et al., 2024), where each example usually admits single correct answer. Aesthetic judgment is different: it is comparative, convention-sensitive, and not reducible to factual correctness alone. Our focus is therefore whether frontier MLLMs can make stable, expert-aligned selections over candidate sets with matched subject matter rather than pointwise judgments on isolated images. Image Aesthetics Assessment. Image aesthetics assessment (IAA) has traditionally been posed as singleimage score prediction. Canonical datasets such as AVA and AADB provide large-scale photographic images with scalar ratings. Later resources extend this paradigm through comments, theme-aware labels, and personalization, including AVA-Reviews, TAD66K, and PARA (Murray et al., 2012; Kong et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2019; He et al., 2022; Yang et al., 2022). These resources support broad line of regression and ranking models, including NIMA, A-Lamp, theme-aware predictors, and more recent vision-language scorers such as Q-Align and UniQA (Talebi and Milanfar, 2018; Ma et al., 2017; He et al., 2022; Wu et al., 2023; Zhou et al., 2024a). Still, the dominant setup remains pointwise, mapping from one image to score, with preference order inferred only indirectly from independent estimates. More recently, MLLM-based efforts have moved toward richer aesthetic reasoning. AesBench evaluates aesthetic perception in frontier MLLMs; AesExpert and UNIAA build instruction-tuned systems for critique and assessment; and ArtiMuse and the photography-focused The Photographers Eye move closer to expertstyle analysis (Huang et al., 2024b;a; Zhou et al., 2024b; Cao et al., 2025; Qi et al., 2025). Nevertheless, existing resources remain largely single-image, score-centric, or domain-specific. In contrast, VA is explicitly set-based: each example is group with matched subject matter, and the task is to identify the strongest and weakest images under controlled content. This formulation targets relative aesthetic discrimination directly, derives labels from expert consensus rather than crowd averages, and spans fine art, photography, and illustration within unified benchmark.

We commissioned 426 painting sets comprising 1,142 images across nine fine-art topics: calligraphy, Chinese painting, ink and wash, landscape color, portrait color, portrait sketch, quick sketch, still-life color, and still-life sketch. For each topic, artists received constrained prompts that fixed the subject and high-level compositional requirements while allowing variation in execution. The resulting sets preserve semantic intent while differing in composition, value structure, color harmony, and paint handling. All works were commissioned specifically for VAB rather than drawn from existing collections, which reduces the risk of contamination from publicly indexed art. Before annotation, we remove near-duplicates and discard sets with semantic mismatches or trivial cues. Photography. We constructed 670 photography sets comprising 1,809 images across nine topics: architecture, food and product, landscape, macro, night and astrophotography, portrait, sports, street and city, and wildlife. Each set originates from single source photograph, many provided directly by professional photographers. We transform these source images into controlled comparison sets through two pipelines. In the expert-edit pipeline, photographers and retouchers improve each source image using tools such as Adobe Photoshop and Lightroom (Adobe, 2026b;a), applying recomposition, color and tone correction, and content-aware expansion. The original photograph and its improved variant form pair. For subset of source images, multiple experts independently edit the same photograph, yielding larger candidate sets with matched subject matter and varying aesthetic quality. In the agent-edit pipeline, each source image is first sent to critic model, ArtiMuse (Cao et al., 2025), which produces detailed aesthetic review. The review and the original image are then passed to generalpurpose MLLM, GPT-5 (OpenAI, 2025b), to generate two editing prompts: one for improvement and one for degradation. These prompts, together with the original image, are fed to an image-to-image model, Gemini-3-Pro-Image (Nano Banana Pro) (Google DeepMind, 2025a), to generate an improved and degraded variant. The original, improved, and degraded images form three-image candidate set. All photography sets from both pipelines are deduplicated and reviewed for semantic consistency before annotation. Illustration. We constructed 271 illustration sets comprising 908 images across six topics: anime and manga, comic, concept art, digital and AI art, pixel art, and stylized 3D. The sets are derived from two distinct sources, prompt-based generation and 3D asset rendering. In the generative pipeline, each set originates from structured prompt that specifies subject identity, scene context, composition, camera viewpoint, lighting, and stylistic attributes. For each set, the prompt is instantiated using single text-to-image model, such as Midjourney v7 (Midjourney, 2026), Pixellab.ai (Create M-XL) (PixelLab, 2026), or LlamaGen (Sun et al., 2024), to generate candidate images with matched subject matter and varying aesthetic quality. The prompt templates are provided in Appendix H.2. In the 3D-rendering pipeline, each set is constructed from CC0-licensed assets sourced from Sketchfab (Creative Commons, 2009; Sketchfab, 2026). For each asset, we render multiple images by varying camera viewpoint while keeping object identity, scene semantics, lighting, and background conditions fixed. The resulting renders form candidate sets with matched subject matter and controlled variation in composition and visual presentation. All illustration sets from both pipelines are deduplicated and reviewed for semantic consistency before annotation. BakeAI

sections.title: C Annotation Details
sections.content: C.1 Annotation Rubrics Annotation Details The rubric serves as structured comparison guide rather than separate numerical scoring form. Judges assess each image against domain-specific criteria before making their final comparative selection. The rubrics below describe the criteria used for each domain.

sections.title: Fine Art Rubric
sections.content: Given set of 2 to 6 fine art paintings with the same style, subject matter, and medium, and only subtle differences, determine which work demonstrates greater artistic completeness and more mature aesthetic expression. Core Comparison Criteria 1. Composition and Visual Order Is the primary subject more clearly defined? Does the composition feel more stable or intentionally dynamic? Is the spatial arrangement natural, without unnecessary congestion or emptiness? 2. Color and Tonal Relationships Are the color relationships more harmonious or layered? Is there stronger control over warmth/coolness and light/dark contrast? Does the work avoid muddiness, dullness, or scattered color impressions? 3. Technical Control Are the brushstrokes more confident, fluid, and rhythmic? Are there signs of hesitation, redundancy, or ineffective marks? Does the technique serve the image rather than draw attention to itself? 4. Degree of Completion Are there any areas that appear unfinished or perfunctory? Are edges and transitions handled naturally? Does the work feel resolved, at point where the artist could reasonably stop? 5. Artistic Expression and Overall Character Is the mood or atmosphere more focused and coherent? Does the work convey stronger overall character or presence? Does it have greater artistic persuasiveness? 6. Final Selection Considering the set as whole, which image is the strongest work? If the set contains three or more images, which image is the weakest work?

sections.title: Should Not Be Considered
sections.content: Personal preference for subject matter. Any contextual or background information beyond what is visible in the image itself. 21 BakeAI

sections.title: Photography Rubric
sections.content: C Annotation Details Given set of 2 to 6 photographs taken of similar subjects, in similar scenes, and under closely matched conditions, determine which image is more successful in terms of photographic language and visual expression. Core Comparison Criteria 1. Composition and Image Structure Is the subject more clearly emphasized? Is the frame more balanced or intentionally dynamic? Is the background cleaner and more supportive of the subject? 2. Quality of Light Is the light more natural or more expressive? Are highlights and shadows better controlled? Does the image avoid crushed blacks or blown highlights? 3. Sharpness and Focus Is focus more accurate where it matters? Is the relationship between sharpness and blur more appropriate? Are there any technical errors that detract from the image? 4. Color or Tonal Rendering Is white balance or color temperature more accurate or more intentional? Are colors or tonal values more consistent and unified? Does the image avoid unwanted color casts or muddy tones? 5. Moment and Emotional Expression Does the image capture stronger or more meaningful moment? Is the emotional or narrative content more compelling? Does it carry stronger sense of photographic presence? 6. Final Selection Considering the set as whole, which photograph is the strongest image? If the set contains three or more images, which photograph is the weakest image?

sections.title: Should Not Be Considered
sections.content: The personal appeal or attractiveness of the subject. Assumptions about shooting difficulty. Assumptions about post-processing effort.

sections.title: Digital Illustration Rubric
sections.content: Given set of 2 to 6 digital illustrations with similar purpose, style, and level of completion, determine which image demonstrates stronger visual design and higher professional illustration quality. Core Comparison Criteria 1. Visual Focus and Information Clarity Is the visual focal point clearer? Does the visual flow guide the viewer more effectively? Is there less visual noise or competition for attention? BakeAI Annotation Details 2. Form and Proportion Are the structures of figures or objects more accurate? Are poses and silhouettes more aesthetically refined? Does the image avoid stiffness, distortion, or unnatural forms? 3. Color Design Is the color palette more unified and thematically coherent? Are value and lighting hierarchies clearer? Is there effective use of contrast and visual rhythm? 4. Detail Control and Finish Are details handled appropriately, neither excessive nor careless? Are materials and lighting consistent throughout? Are edges, masks, and gradients clean and well controlled? 5. Stylistic Consistency and Purpose Fit Does the illustration better align with its intended stylistic direction? Is quality more consistent within unified style? Is the image more suitable for its potential use case? 6. Final Selection Considering the set as whole, which illustration is the strongest work? If the set contains three or more images, which illustration is the weakest work?

sections.title: Should Not Be Considered
sections.content: Personal preference for illustration style. Software or tools used. Hypothetical or imagined client requirements. 23 BakeAI Ground Truth Mathematical Details Table 13: Consensus thresholds and corresponding null pass probabilities by candidate set size k, with = 10 judges per task. 2 3 5 6 Threshold paccept(k, m) 8 7 6 6 10.94% 0.88% 0.95% 0.15% 0.03% C.2 Annotation Interface Figure 5 shows an example annotation interface used by expert judges. Figure 5: The annotation interface presents all candidate images side by side. Judges first review each image with respect to the rubric criteria, then make final comparative selection: selecting the best image for = 2 and selecting both the best and worst images for 3.

sections.title: D Ground Truth Mathematical Details
sections.content: This appendix provides additional mathematical details for the consensus filter described in Section 3.3. D.1 Selected Thresholds Table 13 reports the consensus thresholds used in the benchmark together with their corresponding null pass probabilities. D.2 Derivation of Null Pass Probabilities For 3 candidates with = 10 judges, each judges response is modeled as directed edge (selecting image as best and image as worst, with = j) drawn uniformly from k(k 1) possibilities. Let cij denote the count of judges choosing the pair (i, j). The probability of any configuration under the random null is: Pr(C) = n! i=j cij! (cid:18) 1 k(k 1) (cid:19)n . The exact by-chance pass probability is computed by summing over all configurations satisfying the consensus threshold: paccept(k, m) = C: cii=0, i=j cij=n n! i=j cij! Exact MC Estimate 95% CI 2 3 4 5 6 7 6 6 6 10.94% 10.9496% [10.9303%, 10.9690%] 0.88% 0.95% 0.15% 0.03% 0.8818% 0.9507% 0.1489% 0.0307% [0.8760%, 0.8876%] [0.9447%, 0.9567%] [0.1465%, 0.1513%] [0.0296%, 0.0318%] where Bi = j=i cij (votes for as best) and Wj = i=j cij (votes for as worst). The exact probabilities were computed by exhaustive enumeration over all valid count configurations = (cij) satisfying cii = 0 and i=j cij = n, summing the multinomial probability mass over the configurations that satisfy the acceptance condition. For 3, this acceptance condition is conjunctive: task passes only if the best-vote threshold and the worst-vote threshold are both met, so satisfying only one of the two is insufficient. D.3 Monte Carlo Verification We verify the exact probabilities via Monte Carlo simulation with 107 samples per (k, m) configuration. Table 14 compares the exact values from Table 13 against Monte Carlo estimates and reports 95% Wald confidence intervals. D.4 Special Case for Binary Tasks For binary tasks (k = 2), the worst image is implied once the best is selected. The consensus filter reduces to requiring maxi Bi where Binomial(n, 1/2): paccept(2, m) = Pr(B m) + Pr(B m) = 2 b=m (cid:18)n (cid:19) (cid:18) 1 2 (cid:19)n . For = 10 and = 8, this gives paccept(2, 8) = 2 (45 + 10 + 1)/1024 = 112/1024 10.94%. We use this lower binary threshold because = 2 tasks provide only single comparative decision per annotator; compared with 3, demanding the same level of concentration would remove disproportionate number of otherwise informative binary comparisons.

We retain the same evaluation focus as in the main paper: overall performance relative to expert baselines, cross-domain variation, sensitivity to candidate order, and auxiliary analyses on decoding and task size. E.1 Comprehensive MLLM Results Table 15 reports comprehensive MLLM results, including Bot-1, across overall and domain-level evaluations. These results complement Table 4 in the main text by making the asymmetry between identifying the strongest and weakest images explicit. They also make clear that the gap between ap@1 and pass^3 is pervasive rather than concentrated in small subset of models, consistent with the interpretation in Section 4.2.1 that many MLLM predictions remain sensitive to candidate order and response instability. The full table also makes several model-level patterns easier to inspect. Finally, the domain columns reinforce the main-text headline that illustration is the most difficult domain, photography is generally more tractable, and fine art lies between them. E.2 Comprehensive Reward-Model Results Table 16 reports comprehensive reward-model results, including Bot-1, across overall and domain-level evaluations. In contrast to the condensed main-text presentation in Table 5, this table shows that some reward models are materially stronger at rejecting poor images than at identifying the best one. In particular, the detailed Bot-1 columns make the failure mode discussed in Section 4.2.2 directly visible, especially for Q-Instruct in photography. E.3 Topic-Level MLLM Results To match the main-text MLLM table, we report topic-level results under all four metrics: Top-1 ap@1, Top-1 pass^3, TB-1 ap@1, and TB-1 pass^3. Together, these tables show that the domain-level trends in Section 4.2.1 are not driven by single topic or single evaluation metric. Illustration remains the hardest domain across all four views, while photography is generally more tractable but still highly uneven across topics. Several topic effects recur across metrics. In fine art, Chinese painting and portrait-color tasks are often relatively tractable, whereas landscape-color and some sketch-based tasks become much less stable under the stricter criteria. In illustration, the remaining signal is concentrated mostly in anime-and-manga and comic tasks, while concept art, digital-and-AI art, and stylized 3D frequently collapse under pass^3. In photography, macro and sports tasks are often close to saturation for strong models, but night-andastrophotography, architecture, and some street-and-city tasks remain much less stable, especially when consistency across repeated evaluations is required. As in the MLLM case, the domain-level averages in the main text mask substantial variation across topics. The topic-level reward-model tables show that relatively stronger domain-level results in photography are driven by subset of topics, while illustration remains difficult across most topics for all six reward models. The topic breakdown also clarifies where reward models do and do not transfer. Fine-art performance is mixed but not uniformly weak, with several models reaching competitive results on Chinese painting and portrait-color tasks. Illustration remains systematically difficult: outside anime-and-manga and, in few cases, comic, most models stay close to random on concept art, digital-and-AI art, pixel art, and stylized 3D, especially under TB-1. Photography remains the strongest domain, but the gains are concentrated in subset of topics and do not transfer uniformly to the harder best-and-worst selection setting. Top-1. sections.title: F Human Study Details
sections.content: F.1 Detailed Setup and Metric Definitions This subsection provides the full metric definitions summarized in Section 2. Eight expert annotators evaluate identical images under both protocols. Under scoring, each annotator independently assigns an absolute score si,a [0, 10] to each image. Under ranking, annotators provide complete within-task ordering when presented with groups of size {2, 3, 4, 5}. We operationalize more scientifically sound along three dimensions: fidelity to human comparative preference, reproducibility across annotators, and sharpness of the resulting label signal. The study comprises 119 homogeneous-content tasks drawn directly from the curated benchmark, where all candidates share the same underlying content, and 107 heterogeneous-content tasks formed by regrouping 370 images from distinct homogeneous task groups while preserving topic consistency. Each image appears in at most one regrouped task. Examples of both settings are given in Appendix F.3. Fidelity. We ask whether the ranking induced by absolute scores matches the direct ranking produced by the same annotator. Let ρsame denote annotator as direct rankings for homogeneous-content and heterogeneous-content tasks, and let σscore denote the ranking induced by the absolute scores. We report Kendalls τ (Kendall, 1938), and ρdiff τ = (n 2) , where and are the numbers of concordant and discordant pairs. We compute τ for each annotator within each task, average across annotators to obtain task-level value, and report the mean and standard deviation across tasks. We additionally report top-1 self-consistency (SC) rate: the fraction of annotatortask pairs for which σscore and the direct ranking select the same top image. Reproducibility. We define task-level accuracy acc as the fraction of tasks on which at least five of the eight annotators agree on the relevant label, and report accbest, accworst, and accbest&worst for both protocols. 38 BakeAI Human Study Details Homogeneous-Content Example Heterogeneous-Content Example Group size: = 4 Topic: Photography / Architecture Construction: All four candidates come from the same source task and share the same underlying scene content. Group size: = 4 Topic: Digital Illustration / Pixel Art Construction: Candidates are regrouped from different source tasks while preserving topic consistency. same facade warm light, clean framing same facade flat light, weaker balance source task 014 alley scene source task 037 rooftop scene same facade tighter crop, stronger depth same facade cluttered background source task 081 market scene source task 104 tower scene Scoring protocol. Each image is shown independently and receives separate score in [0, 10]. Ranking protocol. The full set is shown jointly and ranked from best to worst. Scoring protocol. The same images are scored one by one, without seeing the regrouped set. Ranking protocol. Annotators jointly compare the regrouped candidates and produce full ordering. Figure 6: Schematic examples of the two settings used in the human study. Left: homogeneous-content task, where all candidates come from the same source task and share the same underlying content. Right: heterogeneous-content task, where candidates are regrouped from different source tasks. In both settings, the scoring protocol rates images independently, whereas the ranking protocol orders the full set jointly. Distinguishability. Restricting to the 119 homogeneous-content tasks, where entropy reflects concentration of aesthetic preference rather than content mismatch, we measure the entropy of the eight annotators top-1 selections (Shannon, 1948), = where pi is the fraction of annotators selecting image as the top image in task. Because the maximum entropy depends on task size n, we report the unnormalized quantity so that lower values directly indicate stronger concentration of top-choice judgments. pi log2 pi, F.2 Extended Interpretation Philosophical lens. Our results echo the long-standing debate between Aesthetic Absolutism, which posits context-free, intrinsic standard of beauty, and Aesthetic Relativism, which holds that judgments arise from comparison and context. Modern philosophical aesthetics has largely moved toward the relational view: people can reliably tell which image they prefer when alternatives are juxtaposed. Statistical lens. Scoring introduces an additional measurement layer. Annotators must first form an internal judgment and then translate it onto numeric scale, after which pairwise preference is inferred from score differences. This pipeline adds calibration variance, scale-use heterogeneity, and extra measurement noise. Ranking measures the relative decision of interest more directly, which helps explain its higher consistency and sharper signal. Cognitive lens. Comparative judgment is natural human operation. People can often say which of two images feels better before they can specify by how much. Absolute scoring is cognitively heavier because it requires maintaining an internal reference scale, calibrating intervals, and converting qualitative impression into number. That added abstraction likely makes scoring less stable than direct comparison. 39 BakeAI F.3 Task Examples Human Study Details Figure 6 provides schematic examples of the two task settings used in the human study. F.4 Annotation Protocol Scoring task. Under the scoring protocol, each annotator independently assigned an absolute score si,a [0, 10] to each of the 400 images in the base image pool. Images were presented individually, without access to the other members of the same task group. The resulting scores were later reused to derive score-derived rankings for both the 119 homogeneous-content tasks and the 107 heterogeneous-content regroupings. Domain-specific single-image scoring rubrics are given below. Fine Art Single-Image Scoring Rubric For single fine art painting, evaluate the degree of artistic completeness and maturity of aesthetic expression based solely on the image itself, and assign an overall score from 0 to 10, accurate to one decimal place. Reference Scoring Criteria 1. Composition and Visual Order Is the primary subject clearly defined and visually grounded? Does the composition feel stable or intentionally dynamic? Is the spatial arrangement natural, without unnecessary congestion or emptiness? 2. Color and Tonal Relationships Are color relationships harmonious and layered? Is there effective control of warmth/coolness and light/dark contrast? Does the work avoid muddiness, dullness, or scattered color impressions? 3. Technical Control Are the brushstrokes confident, fluid, and rhythmic? Are there signs of hesitation, redundancy, or ineffective marks? Does technique serve the image rather than draw attention to itself? 4. Degree of Completion Are there areas that appear unfinished or perfunctory? Are edges and transitions handled naturally? Does the work feel resolved, at point where the artist could reasonably stop? 5. Artistic Expression and Overall Character Is the mood or atmosphere focused and coherent? Does the work convey unified overall character or presence? Does it demonstrate strong artistic persuasiveness? 6. Overall Score Based on all criteria above, assign an overall score from 0 to 10, accurate to one decimal place.

sections.title: Should Not Be Considered
sections.content: Personal preference for subject matter. Artist background, intent, or any contextual information beyond what is visible in the image itself. 40 BakeAI Human Study Details Photography Single-Image Scoring Rubric For single photograph, evaluate its overall quality in terms of photographic language and visual expression, based solely on the image itself, and assign an overall score from 0 to 10, accurate to one decimal place. Reference Scoring Criteria 1. Composition and Image Structure Is the subject clearly emphasized? Does the frame feel balanced or intentionally dynamic? Is the background clean and supportive of the subject? 2. Quality of Light Is the light natural or expressive? Are highlights and shadows well controlled? Does the image avoid crushed blacks or blown highlights? 3. Sharpness and Focus Is focus accurate where it matters most? Is the relationship between sharpness and blur appropriate? Are there any noticeable technical flaws? 4. Color or Tonal Rendering Is white balance or color temperature accurate or clearly intentional? Are colors or tonal values consistent and unified? Does the image avoid unwanted color casts or muddy tones? 5. Moment and Emotional Expression Does the image capture meaningful moment? Is the emotional or narrative content clear and compelling? Does the image convey strong sense of photographic presence? 6. Overall Score Based on all criteria above, assign an overall score from 0 to 10, accurate to one decimal place.

sections.title: Should Not Be Considered
sections.content: The personal appeal or attractiveness of the subject. Assumptions about shooting difficulty. Assumptions about post-processing effort. Digital Illustration Single-Image Scoring Rubric For single digital illustration, evaluate its overall quality in terms of visual design and professional illustration standards, based solely on the image itself, and assign an overall score from 0 to 10, accurate to one decimal place. Reference Scoring Criteria 1. Visual Focus and Information Clarity Is the visual focal point clear and well defined? Does the visual flow guide the viewer smoothly? Is there minimal visual noise or competition for attention? 41 BakeAI Human Study Details 2. Form and Proportion Are the structures of figures or objects accurate? Are poses and silhouettes aesthetically refined? Does the image avoid stiffness, distortion, or unnatural forms? 3. Color Design Is the color palette unified and thematically coherent? Are value and lighting hierarchies clear? Is contrast and visual rhythm used effectively? 4. Detail Control and Finish Are details handled appropriately, neither excessive nor careless? Are materials and lighting consistent throughout the image? Are edges, masks, and gradients clean and well controlled? 5. Stylistic Consistency and Purpose Fit Does the illustration align with its intended stylistic direction? Is quality consistent within unified style? Is the image suitable for its potential use case? 6. Overall Score Based on all criteria above, assign an overall score from 0 to 10, accurate to one decimal place.

sections.title: Should Not Be Considered
sections.content: Personal preference for illustration style. Software or tools used. Hypothetical or imagined client requirements. When converting absolute scores into induced rankings, no exact within-task score ties were observed in the collected annotations, so no tie-breaking rule was required. Ranking task. Under the ranking protocol, each annotator provided complete best-to-worst ordering of all images presented simultaneously within group. Annotators were instructed to rank the images by overall aesthetic quality using the following domain-specific comparative rubrics.

sections.title: Fine Art Ranking Rubric
sections.content: For set of fine art paintings that share the same style, subject matter, and medium, and differ only subtly in execution, rank the images from best to worst according to artistic completeness and maturity of aesthetic expression. Reference Comparison Criteria 1. Composition and Visual Order Is the primary subject more clearly defined? Does the composition feel more stable or intentionally dynamic? Is the spatial arrangement natural, without unnecessary congestion or emptiness? 2. Color and Tonal Relationships Are the color relationships more harmonious or layered? Is there stronger control over warmth/coolness and light/dark contrast? 42 BakeAI Human Study Details Does the work avoid muddiness, dullness, or scattered color impressions? 3. Technical Control Are the brushstrokes more confident, fluid, and rhythmic? Are there signs of hesitation, redundancy, or ineffective marks? Does the technique serve the image rather than draw attention to itself? 4. Degree of Completion Are there any areas that appear unfinished or perfunctory? Are edges and transitions handled naturally? Does the work feel resolved, at point where the artist could reasonably stop? 5. Artistic Expression and Overall Character Is the mood or atmosphere more focused and coherent? Does the work convey stronger overall character or presence? Does it have greater artistic persuasiveness? 6. Final Selection Considering the set as whole, determine the relative ordering of the images from strongest to weakest.

sections.title: Should Not Be Considered
sections.content: Personal preference for subject matter. Any contextual or background information beyond what is visible in the image itself.

sections.title: Photography Ranking Rubric
sections.content: For set of photographs taken of similar subjects, in similar scenes, and under closely matched conditions, rank the images from best to worst in terms of photographic language and visual expression. Reference Comparison Criteria 1. Composition and Image Structure Is the subject more clearly emphasized? Is the frame more balanced or intentionally dynamic? Is the background cleaner and more supportive of the subject? 2. Quality of Light Is the light more natural or more expressive? Are highlights and shadows better controlled? Does the image avoid crushed blacks or blown highlights? 3. Sharpness and Focus Is focus more accurate where it matters? Is the relationship between sharpness and blur more appropriate? Are there any technical errors that detract from the image? 4. Color or Tonal Rendering Is white balance or color temperature more accurate or more intentional? Are colors or tonal values more consistent and unified? Does the image avoid unwanted color casts or muddy tones? BakeAI Human Study Details 5. Moment and Emotional Expression Does the image capture stronger or more meaningful moment? Is the emotional or narrative content more compelling? Does it carry stronger sense of photographic presence? 6. Final Selection Considering the set as whole, determine the relative ordering of the photographs from strongest to weakest.

sections.title: Should Not Be Considered
sections.content: The personal appeal or attractiveness of the subject. Assumptions about shooting difficulty. Assumptions about post-processing effort.

sections.title: Digital Illustration Ranking Rubric
sections.content: For set of digital illustrations with similar purpose, style, and level of completion, rank the images from best to worst according to visual design and professional illustration quality. Reference Comparison Criteria 1. Visual Focus and Information Clarity Is the visual focal point clearer? Does the visual flow guide the viewer more effectively? Is there less visual noise or competition for attention? 2. Form and Proportion Are the structures of figures or objects more accurate? Are poses and silhouettes more aesthetically refined? Does the image avoid stiffness, distortion, or unnatural forms? 3. Color Design Is the color palette more unified and thematically coherent? Are value and lighting hierarchies clearer? Is there effective use of contrast and visual rhythm? 4. Detail Control and Finish Are details handled appropriately, neither excessive nor careless? Are materials and lighting consistent throughout? Are edges, masks, and gradients clean and well controlled? 5. Stylistic Consistency and Purpose Fit Does the illustration better align with its intended stylistic direction? Is quality more consistent within unified style? Is the image more suitable for its potential use case? 6. Final Selection Considering the set as whole, determine the relative ordering of the illustrations from strongest to weakest.

F.6 Statistical Tests We report two-sided paired tests over tasks to assess whether the ranking and scoring protocols differ systematically. Unless otherwise noted, p-values are uncorrected for multiple comparisons. McNemars test for majority-agreement comparisons. For each task and each metric accbest, accworst, and accbest&worst, we construct paired binary outcomes indicating whether the protocol achieves five-of-eight majority hit. We then apply an exact two-sided McNemar test (McNemar, 1947; Fagerland et al., 2013) to the discordant pairs. sections.title: H Prompt Templates and Scoring Interfaces
sections.content: This appendix records the prompt templates and scoring interfaces used in data construction and evaluation. We group them by usage: prompts for the photography pipeline, prompts for the illustration pipeline, prompts for MLLM evaluation, and scoring interfaces for reward-model evaluation. H.1 Photography Pipeline Prompts This subsection records the prompts used in the photography agent-edit pipeline described in Section 3. The pipeline consists of three stages: aspect-wise critique generation with ArtiMuse, edit planning with GPT through LiteLLM, and image editing with Gemini-3-Pro-Image (Nano Banana Pro). In Step 1, ArtiMuse is queried eight times independently with the same image, once per aesthetic aspect. The resulting responses are then concatenated into single review string that is passed to the planning stage. H.1.1 Step 1: ArtiMuse Aspect-Wise Critique ArtiMuse Aspect-Wise Critique Calls The same image is attached in every call. The following eight prompts are sent independently: 1. Please evaluate the aesthetic quality of this image from the aspect of Composition & Design. 2. Please evaluate the aesthetic quality of this image from the aspect of Visual Elements & Structure. 3. Please evaluate the aesthetic quality of this image from the aspect of Technical Execution. 4. Please evaluate the aesthetic quality of this image from the aspect of Originality & Creativity. 5. Please evaluate the aesthetic quality of this image from the aspect of Theme & Communication. 6. Please evaluate the aesthetic quality of this image from the aspect of Emotion & Viewer Response. 7. Please evaluate the aesthetic quality of this image from the aspect of Overall Gestalt. 8. Please evaluate the aesthetic quality of this image from the aspect of Comprehensive Evaluation.

sections.title: Critique Aggregation Format
sections.content: The eight responses are concatenated into single review string in the following format: [Composition & Design] <response> [Visual Elements & Structure] <response> [Technical Execution] <response> [Originality & Creativity] <response> [Theme & Communication] <response> [Emotion & Viewer Response] <response> [Overall Gestalt] <response> [Comprehensive Evaluation] <response> H.1.2 Step 2: Edit Planning The planning stage is executed with GPT through LiteLLM. Below we reproduce the literal runtime system and user messages. The placeholder {review} denotes the full ArtiMuse output obtained by concatenating the eight aspect-wise responses from Step 1. We retain the original runtime field names, including substyle.

sections.title: Planning System Message
sections.content: You are an expert aesthetic analyst and image editing planner for creating aesthetic preference benchmark datasets. 56 BakeAI

sections.title: Planning User Message
sections.content: H Prompt Templates and Scoring Interfaces Analyze this photograph and plan synthetic edit for an aesthetic preference dataset. AESTHETIC ASSESSMENT: {review} AVAILABLE CLASSIFICATIONS: Domains: Photo Substyles: { "Photo": [ "Portrait", "Landscape", "StreetCity", "Architecture", "Wildlife", "Macro", "FoodProduct", "NightAstro", "Sports" ] } TASK: 1. Classify the image: choose the most appropriate domain and substyle based on image itself 2. Freely decide how to edit this image to create better and worse versions 3. Write "better" prompt: describe how to edit THIS image to make it aesthetically better 4. Write "worse" prompt: describe how to edit THIS image to make it aesthetically worse - "Better" means: take the current image and improve its quality - "Worse" means: take the current image and degrade its quality. EDIT Types - Do NOT always default to color/tone adjustments. Actively consider all edit types: 1. **Color & Tonal Adjustments**: - White balance, color cast correction or introduction - Saturation, vibrance, contrast adjustments - Color grading and mood shifts 2. **Perspective & Viewpoint Changes**: - Simulate slightly different camera angle or position - Adjust the apparent depth or distance from the subject - Change the sense of scale or spatial relationships 3. **Element Removal**: - Remove contextual elements that enhance (or clutter) the scene - Modify background elements to improve or degrade composition 4. **Compositional Restructuring**: - Adjust cropping or framing emphasis - Rebalance visual weight distribution - Modify leading lines or focal points 5. **Light & Shadow Changes**: - Modify shadow depth, highlight recovery, or exposure - Change lighting direction or intensity - Adjust local brightness to guide visual attention Choose multiple edit types that best address the specific strengths and weaknesses identified in the aesthetic assessment. WORSE PROMPT RESTRICTIONS: 57 BakeAI Prompt Templates and Scoring Interfaces When creating the "worse" prompt, DO NOT use the following editing techniques: - DO NOT add lens flare or light flares - DO NOT add vignetting (darkened corners) - DO NOT add object outlines, glows, or halos around subjects These techniques are overused and create unrealistic degradation. PROMPT WRITING GUIDELINES: - Describe the scene narratively, dont just list keywords, narrative, descriptive paragraph will almost always produce better, more coherent image than list of disconnected words. - Use photographic and cinematic language terms. Mention camera angles, lens types, lighting, and fine details to guide the model toward photorealistic result. - Explain how changes should integrate into the scene (lighting, style, composition) - For adding/removing elements: describe what to add/remove and how it should blend in - For style changes: describe both the target style and what to preserve Return JSON: { "content": {"domain": "Photo", "substyle": "..."}, "planned_prompts": { "better": "...", "worse": "..." }, "reasoning": "Explain your substyle classification choice and why you chose these specific photographic edits for better/worse versions" } H.1.3 Step 3: Image Editing The final stage uses Gemini-3-Pro-Image (Nano Banana Pro) to synthesize the edited variants. Round 1 generates the better image from the original image alone. Round 2 generates the worse image from the original image while conditioning on the better image as additional reference context. For Round 2, we report the file-backed with-context template actually used at runtime. This is the operative version loaded by the pipeline and therefore excludes the fallback sentence beginning with Important: Apply this edit to the ORIGINAL image that appears only in the default code path. Round 1: Better-Image Editing Prompt You are an expert image editor. Edit the provided image according to these instructions: {edit_instruction} Round 2: Worse-Image Editing Prompt with Context You are an expert image editor. Context: Previously, the following improvement was made to the original image: "{context_prompt}" (The improved result is shown in the second image for reference) Now, edit the ORIGINAL image (first image) according to these instructions: "{edit_instruction}" H.2 Illustration Pipeline Prompts This subsection records the prompt templates used in the prompt-based illustration generation pipeline. We use one template for digital and AI art, concept art, and pixel art, and separate storyboard-style template 58 BakeAI Prompt Templates and Scoring Interfaces for anime and manga and comic generation. The stylized 3D subset is constructed through asset rendering rather than text-to-image prompting. H.2.1 Digital and AI Art / Concept Art / Pixel Art Generation For digital and AI art, concept art, and pixel art generation, we use structured template to control semantic content while allowing variation in visual execution. Each prompt is composed in fixed order: subject or character, motion or action, environment or background, lighting condition, atmosphere or mood, camera or composition, and art style.

sections.title: Illustration Structured Prompt Template
sections.content: Generate 25 concise prompts ready for text-to-image artwork generation on the topic of [ART CATEGORY]. Each prompt should describe clear visual scene using the following order: subject or character, motion, environment/background, lighting condition, atmosphere or mood, camera or composition (view, framing, depth), and art style. H.2.2 Manga / Comic Storyboard Generation For anime and manga and comic generation, we adopt storyboard-style template with stronger structural constraints. Each prompt describes short multi-panel narrative with consistent characters and environment, while each panel specifies shot type, character action, key props, and scene details.

sections.title: Illustration Storyboard Prompt Template
sections.content: Generate 25 concise storyboard prompts for [ART CATEGORY] artwork generation. Each prompt should describe micro-story using panels. Anchors such as character identity and environment must remain consistent. Each panel must specify shot type (wide, medium, close-up), character actions, key props, and environment details. Keep the description visual and concise. H.3 MLLM Evaluation Prompts This subsection reports the generic prompts used to evaluate MLLMs on VA B. Depending on the task setting, the model is asked to identify the best image, the worst image, or both the best and the worst images in candidate set. H.3.1 Best-Image Selection Prompt Pick-Best Prompt You will be shown {n} images labeled {labels}. Evaluate the overall aesthetic quality of each image, considering composition, color, technique, and artistic expression. Which image has the best overall aesthetic quality? {options} You may reason step by step. Then, on the very last line of your response, write your final answer in exactly this format: BEST: where is the letter label of the best image. 59 BakeAI Prompt Templates and Scoring Interfaces H.3.2 Worst-Image Selection Prompt Pick-Worst Prompt You will be shown {n} images labeled {labels}. Evaluate the overall aesthetic quality of each image, considering composition, color, technique, and artistic expression. Which image has the worst overall aesthetic quality? {options} You may reason step by step. Then, on the very last line of your response, write your final answer in exactly this format: WORST: where is the letter label of the worst image. H.3.3 Joint Best-and-Worst Selection Prompt Pick-Best-and-Worst Prompt You will be shown {n} images labeled {labels}. Evaluate the overall aesthetic quality of each image, considering composition, color, technique, and artistic expression. Which image has the best overall aesthetic quality, and which has the worst? {options} You may reason step by step. Then, on the very last lines of your response, write your final answers in exactly this format: BEST: WORST: where and are the letter labels. H.4 Reward-Model Scoring Interfaces This subsection records the model-specific scoring interfaces used to evaluate reward models on VA B. Unlike MLLMs, reward models do not share single comparative prompt. Instead, each model scores images independently following its released evaluation method, and we convert the resulting per-image scores into within-task rankings. Across all reward models, each image is scored num_trials times, the scores are averaged, and the averaged scores are used to determine the predicted best and worst images. We use the following terminology: explicit prompt denotes prompt text exposed at the call site, implicit prompt denotes prompt text embedded inside the model library, and no prompt denotes pure vision scorer with no language instruction. H.4.1 ArtiMuse ArtiMuse uses an implicit prompt: the instruction is hardcoded inside the released library rather than passed from our evaluation script.

sections.title: ArtiMuse Internal Scoring Prompt
sections.content: Rate the aesthetics score of the image in 0-100. In the output format, numbers are replaced by 2 corresponding letters, and the mapping relationship is: score 0 to 25: 0-aa, 1-ab, 2-ac, 3-ad, . . . , 25-az, score 26 to 50: 26-ca, 27-cb, 28-cc, 29-cd, . . . , 50-cy, score 51 to 75: 51-da, 52-db, 53-dc, 54-dd, . . . , 75-dy, score 76 to 100: 76-ea, 77-eb, 78-ec, 79-ed, . . . , 100-ey. 60 BakeAI Prompt Templates and Scoring Interfaces The answer only outputs 2 corresponding letters. At runtime, the library automatically prepends an image token before the instruction. The model outputs two-letter code, which is then decoded back to numerical score in [0, 100]. H.4.2 Q-Align Q-Align also uses an implicit prompt. In our experiments, we use the aesthetic scorer by default; the library additionally exposes quality scorer. Q-Align Internal Prompts Aesthetic scorer (used by default): USER: How would you rate the aesthetics of this image? <image> ASSISTANT: The aesthetics of the image is Quality scorer (alternative interface): USER: How would you rate the quality of this image? <image> ASSISTANT: The quality of the image is The scorer reads the last-token logits over the five words excellent, good, fair, poor, and bad, applies softmax, and computes weighted sum with weights [1, 0.75, 0.5, 0.25, 0]. H.4.3 Q-Instruct Q-Instruct uses an explicit prompt passed through the command-line interface. Q-Instruct Evaluation Prompt Rate the quality of the image. The instruction is wrapped in the mplug_owl2 conversation format with prepended DEFAULT_IMAGE_TOKEN. The default score is the logit difference between good and poor. H.4.4 RealQA RealQA uses an explicit prompt. Under the AVA adapter used in our experiments, the default instruction is:

sections.title: RealQA Evaluation Prompt
sections.content: Rate the aesthetics and quality of this image briefly. The model generates free text, from which numerical score in [0.0, 10.0] is parsed. If parsing fails, the library issues second constrained prompt:

sections.title: RealQA Retry Prompt
sections.content: Output only one numeric score. Range: 0.0 to 10.0. No words, no explanation. H.4.5 PEAS PEAS-aes and PEAS-ava use no prompt. They are pure vision models with no language instruction. The scorer outputs 10-bin distribution over scores from 1 to 10, and the final score is the expected value of that distribution. 61 BakeAI

sections.title: PEAS Scoring Interface
